sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Wayne, presents with a non-healing wound on his right foot, which he has had for approximately two to three weeks.
He reports that the wound did not improve after a course of antibiotics prescribed by his primary care physician, and he is now experiencing a blister-like wound that is stuck to his sock.
He denies any trauma to the foot and is unable to feel the wound due to neuropathy.
He also reports that the wound does not hurt, but he has noticed that it is not healing.
Past medical history: The patient has diabetes, with an HbA1c level of a little over 8, and has a history of neuropathy.
He used to see a podiatrist but was unable to find a new one after they moved.
Review of systems: The patient denies any other symptoms such as fever, chills, or drainage from the wound.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: Temperature 98.1, heart rate 72, respirations 16, blood pressure 110/65.
Physical exam: A 1x2 inch circular wound is present on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, just proximal to the right fifth to the fifth MTP joint.
The wound has yellow slough present with minimal granulation tissue, no surrounding erythema or cellulitis, and no fluid collection.
There is no necrosis, no odor, and no bony exposure.
The vascular exam shows palpable bilateral femoral and popliteal pulses, with no palpable DP or PT pulses, but Doppler signs are present.
Test Results: The right foot X-ray shows no evidence of osteomyelitis.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a diabetic foot ulcer on the right foot, which is not healing despite previous antibiotic treatment.
Problem list: Diabetic foot ulcer; poor diabetic control; neuropathy; potential vascular insufficiency.
Plan:
I prescribed collagenase ointment to be applied to the wound once daily, covered with a dry sterile dressing.
I will perform a debridement in the office to remove dead tissue.
I will order an ankle-brachial index (ABI) to determine the blood supply to the foot.
I will have the patient wear a surgical shoe to take pressure off the area.
I do not think any more antibiotics are needed at this time.
I will refer the patient to a vascular specialist if the ABI indicates poor blood supply for wound healing.
I will see the patient again in two weeks.
Follow-up: I will see the patient in two weeks to assess the wound and determine if further treatment is needed.
